Name: curvature_aware_dynamic_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Dynamic Routing: An Empirical Framework for Latency-Optimized Inference
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic routing of tokens through layers based on curvature diagnostics will lead to significant improvements in latency and accuracy in deep learning models, particularly under strict computational budgets.
Related Work: Existing methods have primarily focused on static optimization or heuristic-based approaches for managing inference latency. Notably, 'Spectral Latent Steering' and 'Curvature-Aware Replay' explore related themes but lack a real-time, dynamic adjustment mechanism based on curvature diagnostics. This proposal introduces a novel mechanism that leverages real-time curvature diagnostics to adaptively route tokens based on their computational needs, bridging a critical gap in existing methodologies.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for dynamic token routing in deep learning models, utilizing curvature-aware diagnostics to optimize inference latency under strict computational constraints. The hypothesis is that the curvature properties of activation distributions can be leveraged to determine the necessity of routing tokens through deeper layers or skipping certain computations altogether. This method quantifies the curvature of activation landscapes using Hessian information and adapts the routing of tokens in real-time, ensuring a balance between accuracy and latency. By implementing this curvature-aware dynamic routing, we aim to improve throughput and maintain model performance in latency-sensitive applications. The framework will be validated on benchmark datasets, showcasing its ability to outperform traditional static routing methods and heuristic approaches. This work contributes to the understanding of curvature in ML optimization while offering a practical solution for enhancing real-time inference efficiency.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline model with static routing and measure inference latency and accuracy under various computational budgets.
2. Incorporate curvature diagnostics into the model to adaptively route tokens, measuring the changes in latency and accuracy across different layers.
3. Evaluate the proposed framework on diverse benchmarks (e.g., image classification, natural language processing) to assess performance improvements.
4. Use metrics such as latency (in ms), accuracy (in %), and throughput (images processed per second) to compare the dynamic routing approach against the static baseline.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The effectiveness of curvature diagnostics in real-time scenarios may vary based on the model architecture.
2. Computational overhead from calculating Hessian information may offset latency gains in some cases.
3. Dynamic routing may introduce instability if not properly calibrated, potentially leading to performance degradation.
